schools.summary: The chariots and fifty runners are the calculated opening of a bid for the throne; Ellicott notes the exact parallel with Adonijah and the first regal use of horses in Israel.
As eldest surviving son Absalom courts the people's eyes; Clarke gathers Calmet's portrait of a bold, vain, popular prince whose crime was to seek the crown in his father's lifetime.
Matthew Henry: Absalom counterfeits greatness and goodness; David's indulgence of a proud son is a fond parent's fatal mistake.
Gill: 'after this' — the very reconciliation lately granted becomes the platform for treason.
Absalom stations himself at the gate, Israel's seat of justice, intercepting every suitor; K&D weighs the emphatic Hebrew wish 'who might appoint me judge.'
Wesley: the early rising and eager greeting are a 'shew of solicitude,' diligence real in labour but hollow in aim.
Gill: calculated industry to ingratiate himself with the people against the day he would be king.
Ellicott: Absalom does not accuse the king directly but insinuates the whole system is defective, the perennial art of the demagogue; officials really were appointed to hear causes.
Wesley: he flatters the suitor and slanders the rulers as corrupt and the king as negligent — the way of aspiring men to reproach the government they are under.
Gill: he pronounces the cause good without hearing the other party — no fit judge at all.
Gill: Absalom speaks to the same persons at the same time — the naked wish 'O that I were a judge' is a deliberate link in the campaign, not a stray complaint.
Gill: Absalom will not let the suitor bow, but takes him by the hand and kisses him as a friend — condescension refusing the distance of rank.
K&D: by exactly such conduct Agamemnon secured command of the Greek army; the affability is the studied instrument that stole the people's hearts.
K&D: 'stole the heart' does not mean to cheat (as in Genesis 31:20) but to win a person secretly and by stratagem — affection quietly diverted from the king.
Clarke: Absalom was versed in the arts of the demagogue, used the patriot's arguments, and only wanted the rulers' place that he might act as they did, or worse.
Gill: by slander, promise, and caress together he won all Israel that came for judgment.
The 'forty years' is judged a corrupt text; K&D, Ellicott (and Clarke) prefer 'four years,' reckoned probably from Absalom's return to Jerusalem.
Clarke marshals the versional evidence for 'four'; Wesley keeps 'forty,' dating it from the founding of the monarchy, about the thirtieth year of David's reign.
Matthew Henry keeps forty years from the change to monarchy, and frankly doubts Absalom ever made the vow — religion made a stalking-horse to sedition.
Gill canvasses every proposal and, finding none satisfactory, suspects a copyist's error of 'arbaim' for 'arba,' forty for four.

debates.question: Is 'after forty years' the original reading, or should it be 'four years'?

sides.position: the text is corrupt; read 'four years' (from Absalom's return)
keep 'forty years,' dated from the founding of the monarchy

themes: textual crux of the forty years

schools.summary: K&D record the vow's terms — 'if the LORD bring me again, I will serve the LORD' — as the plausible cover that wins leave to depart.
Clarke: Geshur lay near Edom, not Syria, so 'Aram' is likely a slip for 'Edom'; and the professed devotion only served to organize the rebellion.
Gill: the vow was made at his grandfather's court in Geshur — the irony left to speak for itself.
Gill: David gave leave and wished him prosperity — a sincere blessing granted to a traitor.
Wesley: Hebron was shrewdly chosen — an eminent city, David's own first capital, near enough to strike and far enough to gather strength, where Absalom had friends.
K&D and Ellicott: the 'spies' first sound each tribe's feeling and raise the cry only where support is sure; the plot had been kept remarkably secret.
Clarke: the trumpet was a kind of telegraph, exciting trumpet from place to place, so all Israel might hear the proclamation in minutes.
Gill: the spies went to sound the people, insinuate Absalom's cause, and seize every chance to recommend him.
Ellicott and K&D: the two hundred were prominent citizens, wholly ignorant, whose presence lent Absalom prestige and betrayed the plot's extreme secrecy.
Clarke takes them for seduced soldiers; Wesley: men of reputation picked to make observers think David himself consented — good men used by designing men.
Gill relays the tradition that the two hundred were gathered two by two — a parable of how conspiracy grows by imperceptible steps.
Ellicott: Ahithophel's defection is unexplained — perhaps the Bathsheba grievance, more likely the alienation of Judah; his treachery and suicide parallel Judas (John 13:18; Psalms 41:9; 55).
Clarke: Absalom got the prime minister to join him; Wesley: the sacrifices were mere pretext to gather great numbers.
Gill relays the tradition that Ahithophel, Bathsheba's grandfather, resented David's sin, and was sought to yield up the king's secrets.
K&D: 'the heart after Absalom' means attachment to him as king; David flees, hearing the calamity Nathan foretold against his house (2 Samuel 12:10-11).
Matthew Henry: the report, made worse in the telling, David readily believed, reproaching his own past confidence — princes must make sure of their subjects.
Clarke lists seven possible causes of the defection, from David's age and lack of judges to God's displeasure and men's love of the rising sun.
Ellicott and K&D read the flight as sound generalship: speed denies Absalom a sudden blow, spares Jerusalem the sword, and gives David's cause time to recover.
Clarke marvels David fled from so strong a city, and answers that he took all from God's hand; Wesley adds he had no provisions for a siege and could raise an army abroad.
Matthew Henry frames the two readings — David flees as a penitent bowing to the rod, and as a politician refusing to make Jerusalem the seat of war.
Gill: David addresses his resolve to the servants and officers still with him in the city.

debates.question: Why does David, holding a strong city, choose to flee?

sides.position: as a penitent submitting to God's chastening hand
as sound military prudence — sparing the city and preserving his forces

schools.summary: Gill: the servants answer unconditionally, ready to do whatever the king appoints, entirely conformable to his pleasure.
K&D: their declared readiness is the hinge — at once the king goes out with all his household in his train.
Matthew Henry: unmoved by Absalom's arts, the servants assure David of inviolable allegiance, cleaving the tighter to a king in his humiliation.
K&D: the king goes out with his family 'at his feet,' halting at the farthest house from the city on the Jericho road, where the company gathered.
Wesley: David went on foot to humble himself and move compassion, and left the concubines supposing their sex and relation would protect them.
Gill: the king's going forth 'determined the case' — the resolve, once acted on, was past recall.
Ellicott: read 'the far house,' a definite spot at the city's edge before the Kidron, where David mustered his forces and arranged the flight.
Clarke: David waited till all his friends were safely out; Wesley reads the distance as convenient, not very far.
Gill hears in the repeated 'the king went forth' the haste and fright of the departure.

themes: the muster at the far house

schools.summary: K&D and Ellicott: the 'Gittites' are David's own six hundred, named from their sojourn in Gath, tracked from the cave of Adullam (1 Samuel 22:1-2) to Ziklag and Hebron — his later mighty men.
Wesley: they are foreigners, Philistines by birth from Gath, won by David to the true religion and proven in valour and fidelity.
Gill leaves the identity open, noting the servants at the king's side and the guards marching before him.

sides.position: David's own veterans, named from their long stay in Gath
native Philistines of Gath, converts to Israel's religion

schools.summary: K&D and Ellicott: Ittai is a Philistine commander lately come over (later trusted with a third of the army, 2 Samuel 18:2); David frees him as a foreigner from a quarrel not his own, without irony.
Wesley reads 'the king' as Absalom now reigning; Clarke adds the tradition that Ittai was a banished son of Achish, he and his six hundred proselytes to Israel.
Gill: Ittai commanded the band of six hundred Gittites.

debates.question: Whom does David mean by 'abide with the king'?

sides.position: whatever king holds Jerusalem — Ittai as a foreigner is free to serve either

schools.summary: K&D: David will not drag a man so newly come into his uncertain wandering; 'grace and truth' names the grace and faithfulness of God (Proverbs 3:3).
Clarke and Wesley read 'mercy and truth be with thee' as prayer — that God would show Ittai mercy and keep his promises to true-hearted proselytes; the 'brethren' are his fellow-Gittites.
Gill: Ittai had come but yesterday, from Gath or a recent expedition — his attachment newly formed.
K&D render the oath — 'whether for death or life, there will thy servant be' — and defend the received Hebrew text of its solemn asseveration.
Gill: Ittai answered with an oath, swearing by the living LORD and the king's life — loyalty that cannot be bought or dismissed.
Wesley: a friend indeed loves at all times; so should we cleave to the Son of David that neither life nor death separate us from his love.

themes: cleaving to the Son of David

schools.summary: K&D: 'go and pass over' means go forward, not pass by; the 'little ones' are Ittai's whole family, brought entire into David's exile as he crosses the Kidron.
Gill: Ittai's resolve fixed, David urges him no more but bids him pass over the brook before him.
Ellicott, K&D, and Clarke fix the Kidron as a winter torrent east of the city between it and the Mount of Olives, on the wilderness road toward Jericho.
Wesley: this is the very brook Christ crossed entering his sufferings (John 18:1) — the rejected David a figure of his greater Son.
Matthew Henry presses the same likeness — the wronged king weeping over the Kidron prefigures the Son of David going to Gethsemane.
Gill: the country people wept aloud to see their king driven into exile by a rebellious son.
Matthew Henry: the priests, unmoved by Absalom's arts, steadily adhere to David and bring the ark to ask counsel of God — friends to the ark in prosperity find it a friend in adversity.
Ellicott: Zadok, in charge of the ark, sets it down while the crowd assembles and Abiathar leads them up the Mount of Olives.
Clarke: God had given the kingdom to David and not deposed him, so the priests accompany him and take the ark, their sacred charge.
Gill names Zadok the priest at the head of the procession.
Gill: the reason is not easy, but David judged the sacred ark would not be violated and was safest in its own tabernacle.
Clarke: great confidence and humility — the ark too precious to expose; David felt more for God's worship than his own safety; Wesley adds he would spare the priests Absalom's rage.
K&D: David puts his fate in believing confidence into the LORD's hand, making his return depend on God's favour, not his own arms.
Matthew Henry: more concerned for the church than himself, David prefers Jerusalem before his chief joy (Psalms 137:6) and longs for God's house above his throne.
Ellicott: David knows he suffers Nathan's sentence and throws himself into God's hands, unwilling to compel the divine blessing by keeping the ark (2 Samuel 24:14).
Gill: 'no delight in thee' means delight in him as king and in temporal prosperity — not God's everlasting love, which stands secure.
Wesley: see God's hand in all that is, and all that shall be in God's hand — the rule of submission drawn from David's 'here am I.'
Ellicott: the Hebrew allows 'art thou a seer?' or 'thou seer'; Zadok is to fulfil a prophet's office, guiding David and reporting Jerusalem's events; 'your two sons' addresses both priests.

Clarke: Zadok, the one organ of worship, need not fear; David wants frequent intelligence through the priests and their sons. Wesley: 'seer' = a discerning man fit for a task needing prudence and secrecy.
Gill: 'seer' means prophet and a knowing man, of more service to David within the city than in the wilderness.

debates.question: Is 'art not thou a seer' a question or an address ('thou seer')?

sides.position: an address — 'thou seer,' with a slight change of vowel

schools.summary: Ellicott weighs 'plain of the wilderness' (margin, versions) against the text's 'the fords,' preferring the latter as more definite and strategic, with the Jordan crossing open; K&D note the priests then carried the ark back.
Gill places the spot toward the wilderness road by which David went after crossing the Kidron.

debates.question: Should the waiting-place read 'the plain of the wilderness' or 'the fords'?

sides.position: 'the plain of the wilderness' (Hebrew margin and versions)
'the fords' (the Hebrew text), more definite and strategic

schools.summary: Gill: Zadok and Abiathar had the ark carried back by the Kohathite Levites whose office it was, attended it, and remained at their assigned post in the city.
K&D: Zadok and Abiathar returned to the city with the ark, closing its brief journey after the king.
Matthew Henry: the priests returned to await the event, stationed in the city with their sons as couriers in full mutual confidence with the king.

themes: the king's eyes in the city

schools.summary: K&D: covered head and bare feet are marks of mourning (Esther 6:12; Ezekiel 24:17); on this ascent David hears of Ahithophel's defection. Clarke adds the covered head is also the condemned man's posture (Esther 7:8).
Matthew Henry: David weeps not from fear but for a son's unkindness and, above all, God's displeasure; his sin ever before him (Psalms 51:3), a wounded conscience making trouble heavy (Psalms 38:4).
Wesley: the bare feet testify to deep sorrow, humiliation, and shame for his sins.
Gill: the Mount of Olives, a mile east of the city, is where great David's Son so often resorted and whence he ascended — the antitype of this weeping king.

Matthew Henry: Ahithophel's brain was the plot's chief danger; David's short, fervent, particular prayer trusts the God who holds all hearts (Job 12:17; Isaiah 3:2-3), yet must be seconded by endeavour.
K&D place the news on the ascent to the high place on Olivet; Ellicott notes a dropped preposition, the Hebrew reading literally 'and David told.'
Clarke: Ahithophel was well versed in affairs, and God alone could confound his devices.
Ellicott: read 'where men worship,' a high place on Olivet's crest; Hushai (from Joshua 16:2) arrives as the beginning of the answer to David's prayer.
Matthew Henry: David worships (perhaps penning Psalm 3), and 'while he was yet speaking, God heard,' sending Hushai — the instrument to befool Ahithophel.
Clarke: David halts to pray even in peril — 'prayer and provender never hinder any man's journey'; Hushai, his particular friend, comes in mourning.
Clarke: Hushai, no warrior but a wise man, is directed to confederate with the priests and use their sons as couriers — of far more use in the city than in the field.
K&D read the verse as opening the full commission: return, feign allegiance, defeat Ahithophel's counsel, and report through the priests and their sons.
Gill: David spoke after hearing the tidings Hushai brought.
Ellicott: David counsels fraud and Hushai accepts it; the narrative states the facts without justifying them, though war's customs and the age's dim moral light temper the blame.
Wesley: the crafty counsel must be reckoned among David's sins — yet God directed the evil advice to a good end.
Matthew Henry cannot see how the gross dissimulation is justified even as a stratagem of war.
Gill: Hushai was to return to Jerusalem, from which he had come.

debates.question: Can David's counsel of deceit to Hushai be justified?

sides.position: partly excusable — the custom of war and the age's slender moral knowledge temper it
no — it is plainly a sin, only overruled by God to a good end

schools.summary: Gill: the priests are to help form schemes opposite to Ahithophel's and receive whatever secrets Hushai gathers from Absalom's court.
K&D: Hushai is to report all he hears of the king's house to Zadok and Abiathar, who will send word on to David through their sons.
Matthew Henry: David recommends Hushai to the priests as fit to be consulted, weaving counsellor and priesthood into one confederacy.
Gill: the two sons were stationed near Jerusalem, not within it, so the couriers could come and go without suspicion.
K&D: the sons are the messengers by whom the priests' intelligence reaches the king; the grammar marks Hushai's entrance and Absalom's arrival as simultaneous.
Wesley: 'there' means near the city, not in it, whence the priests could easily send word.

K&D: the grammar marks Hushai's entrance and Absalom's arrival as simultaneous — the loyal spy in place as the usurper takes the city; Gill reads Hushai's coming as pure obedience to serve David to the uttermost.
Clarke: behold David's vicissitude — the greatest king driven out by his own son and for a time forsaken by God; none so exalted God cannot abase, none so abased he cannot exalt.
Matthew Henry: David's virtues are still a sinner's; only the Son of David was perfect under treachery — we look for a kingdom that cannot be shaken.
